figures.caption: Figure 4: Integrating literature-derived knowledge and metadata enables detection of disease-associated functional alterations in cytomegalovirus (CMV)–positive individuals. A) UMAP representation of the original gene expression data annotated with CMV status metadata and cell-type labels. B) Separation of CMV status based on cell-wise cosine similarity to a disease interaction description, where cells are classified as CMV-associated (“True”) when exceeding the similarity threshold (see Methods Section 5.10.5). Separation is evaluated for cells of separate cell types using the mean similarity difference and p-value from Mann-Whitney U test. Dot size (red) indicates the number of cells per type. C) Differential gene expression (DEG) analysis of disease associated cells ’Memory CD4 T’ cells separated either by similarity scores based on the model embeddings or by the metadata labels. D) Comparison of log fold changes (logFC) and corresponding log-transformed p-values between the two DEG analyses from the two methods of cell separation.

Figure 2: Joint training on cell-derived and literature-derived datasets enables encoder-only language models to capture biologically meaningful structure in joint text-based embeddings. A) UMAP representation of the HIAI dataset based on 1) base model embeddings, 2) model trained solely on literature-derived dataset, 3) model trained on cell- and literaturederived dataset. Cells are colored by ground truth label from the HIAI dataset while the respective cell type labels are shown in black. B) Cosine similarity between cell embeddings and cell-type label embeddings is used to assess alignment. For each cell type, distributions of similarity scores are compared between cells of that type and all other cells. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves are used to evaluate how well similarity scores separate cells of a given type from others using threshold decision tests. C) Overview of similarity analysis metrics showing the area under the ROC curve (AUC) and MannWhitney U test p-values for all cell types. D) Cosine similarity of cells to labels can be used for cell-type annotation: assigning each cell the cell-type label with the highest similarity yields a confusion matrix comparing predicted and true cell types. E) UMAP representations colored by the predicted vs true cell-type annotations illustrate the alignment between the learned embedding structure and the ground truth.

Figure 5: Model trained on datasets incorporating temporal metadata captures developmental patterns. A) Lineage trajectory from ‘Dorsal forebrain’ to ‘Cortical or hippocampal glutamatergic’ and ‘Forebrain GABAergic’ colored by ground truth cell-type label. UMAP plots use model embeddings and gene expression matrix respectively. B) Comparison of ranked pseudotime results on model embeddings and gene expression data colored by ground truth cell type C) UMAP representations of the original gene expression matrix (top row) and the model embeddings (bottom row) colored by pseudotime values calculated based on 1) model embeddings, 2) gene expression data, and by 3) embryonic day (metadata).

Figure 3: Models trained on cell- and literature-based datasets capture functionality descriptions at both the cell-type and single-cell level. A) Summary table of expert-curated cell-type functionality descriptions. B) Heatmap of cosine similarities between model-derived embeddings of functionality descriptions and corresponding cell-type label embeddings. C) Comparison of our encoder-only models with existing generalist and specialist models on the single-cell and cell-type label-based level, using rank-based association of functionality descriptions with expert-assigned cell-type labels. D) Example application: similarity scores to the functionality annotation ‘cytotoxic’ identify cytotoxic cells. E) Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) of cells annotated as cytotoxic (’True’) to non-cytotoxic cells (’False’), confirming enrichment of known cytotoxicity markers.

Figure 1: Overview of the approach for adding contextual layers of information to scRNA-seq datasets. Left: A conventional single-cell RNA-seq workflow processes a gene expression matrix through a quantitative pipeline comprising dimensionality reduction and differential expression analysis for annotation of cell-type clusters. 1) In our approach, we translate single-cell profiles into cell sentences by ranking highly expressed genes and appending metadata for generating the cell-derived dataset (A). In parallel, we build a literature-derived dataset by retrieving PubMed titles and abstracts related to the known or suspected cell types, capturing complementary information such as cell-type functionality (function), disease relationships (disease) or lineage trajectories (time). For both datasets, we construct label-aware triplets. 2) On the triplets, we train models with a Siamese-BERT [17] architecture, using 3) contrastive fine-tuning to align the embeddings of single cells, cell-type labels and literature-derived information. 4) The resulting joint embedding space enables context-aware analysis of scRNA-seq data with additional layers of biological information captured in the biomedical literature.
